content:
13 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

It’s 11pm and you need a database backup running on the cluster. You’ve shipped a hundred Deployments by now, so you reach for the muscle memory: write a Deployment, point it at a backup script, kubectl apply. Easy.
The pod comes up. The script dumps your database to object storage. The script finishes. The container exits with code 0, a clean, successful exit.
And then Kubernetes does the one thing it’s best at.
It notices the container stopped, decides that’s wrong, and starts it again.
The backup runs again. Same database, same dump, same upload. Exit 0. Restarted again. And again. By the time you check the dashboard, you’ve got a pod stuck in a tight loop, a storage bill climbing by the minute, and a very confused on-call teammate asking why the backup ran 400 times.
Nothing is broken. Kubernetes is doing exactly what you told it to. The problem is the assumption baked into a Deployment:
Applications should never stop running. If one exits, bring it back.
That assumption is perfect for an API. It is catastrophically wrong for a backup. Because some workloads aren’t supposed to run forever — they’re supposed to run, finish, and stay finished.
